skill.name: linux-production-shell-scripts
skill.description: Provides Bash script templates for Linux administration tasks such as backups, monitoring, user management, log analysis, networking, and automation.
skill.summary: Create and adapt Linux shell script templates for common production administration workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell command, filesystem, network, sudo, and cron patterns in SKILL.md. Review confirms these are Bash templates in documentation, not auto-executing code or prompt injection, but several examples can change packages, services, cron jobs, files, accounts, and remote systems if copied and run without review.

medium_findings.title: Privileged System Administration Templates
medium_findings.description: The examples include sudo package installation and sudo service start or restart commands. This is legitimate for Linux administration, but it can alter host state if run unchanged.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands are explicit and affect packages or services. Confidence is high, but the code is presented as a template rather than auto-executed logic.

medium_findings.title: Scheduled Task and Background Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill shows crontab modification and nohup background execution. These patterns can create persistence, although the surrounding context is routine scheduling guidance.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Cron and nohup are directly present at the cited lines. The intent appears administrative, so this is a warning rather than evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Destructive File Operation Templates
medium_findings.description: Backup rotation, directory synchronization, and cleanup examples use rm, rsync --delete, and find -exec rm. Incorrect paths or placeholders could delete important data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deletion commands are concrete and would remove files if executed. The examples quote variables, but they still require careful operator review.

medium_findings.title: Remote and Network Command Templates
medium_findings.description: The examples include rsync to a remote server, public connectivity checks, curl website checks, and SSH remote script execution. These can transfer data or run commands on remote hosts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network commands and remote execution are explicitly shown. The endpoints are placeholders or public test hosts, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

medium_findings.title: System and Account Reconnaissance Templates
medium_findings.description: Several templates collect host, process, account, network, and login information. This is useful for administration but can expose sensitive operational details in reports.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The templates read system status, account names, and host details. The context is reporting, so this is a controlled information disclosure risk.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Credential Handling Examples
medium_findings.description: The database backup template uses a password variable on the mysqldump command line, and the password generator prints the generated secret. Users should replace these patterns before production use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The password handling is visible in the examples and can leak through shell history, process listings, logs, or terminal capture. No exfiltration endpoint is present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Public Network Targets
low_findings.description: The network check examples use public IP addresses and websites. These are common connectivity probes, but users should replace them with approved internal endpoints when required.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The targets are well-known public connectivity examples and no credential or file upload is shown. They still create outbound traffic if the template is run.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptography, but the reviewed encryption example uses openssl AES-256-CBC with PBKDF2 and the password generator uses openssl rand. Authenticated encryption would be stronger.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited cryptographic examples are not clearly weak malware indicators. The file encryption mode lacks authentication, so human review is still appropriate for production guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: sudo Privilege Escalation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Templates use sudo to install packages and control services. These commands should require explicit user approval before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sudo invocations are direct and affect privileged host resources. The skill does not execute them automatically.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cron Modification for Persistence
dangerous_patterns.description: The cron setup example pipes an added schedule into crontab. This is normal automation but also a persistence mechanism if abused.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The crontab write is explicit and changes scheduled execution. The surrounding text describes a routine task scheduler.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive and Pattern-Based Deletion
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include rm -r and find -exec rm for cleanup and rotation. These can destroy data if variables or paths are wrong.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The deletion commands are executable shell patterns. They are expected in cleanup templates but need safeguards before production use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Execution Over SSH
dangerous_patterns.description: The SSH example runs a local script on a remote server. This is powerful administration behavior and should be limited to trusted hosts and reviewed scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command directly executes script input through ssh. Variables are quoted, but the operation is inherently sensitive.

content.value_statement: Linux operators often need repeatable scripts for backups, monitoring, account checks, and routine automation. This skill provides editable Bash templates that Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can adapt to a target environment.
actual_capabilities: Provides Bash templates for backups, monitoring, user management, log analysis, networking, and automation.
Shows tar, rsync, mysqldump, and backup rotation patterns for common backup workflows.
Includes CPU, disk, health check, and system information collection examples.
Covers account creation, password expiry checks, password generation, and file encryption examples.
Provides cron scheduling, package installation, service restart, cleanup, and Git update templates.
Includes a quick reference for common Bash syntax, execution commands, and cron timing.
limitations: Templates must be reviewed and tested before use on production systems.
Several examples require root privileges, installed utilities, or distribution-specific commands.
The templates do not include complete error handling, tests, or rollback logic for every task.
Some examples can expose system details or delete data if placeholders are not changed carefully.

use_cases.title: Create Backup Automation
use_cases.description: Adapt tar, rsync, database backup, and rotation templates for repeatable server backup jobs.

use_cases.title: Draft Monitoring Scripts
use_cases.description: Generate starting points for CPU, disk, service, and system health checks with clear output files.

use_cases.title: Prepare Operations Runbooks
use_cases.description: Use the examples to document safe shell procedures for cleanup, cron scheduling, and service control.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Backup Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Linux production shell scripts skill to create a safe backup script for this source and destination. Include comments, input variables, and a dry-run option.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt a Monitoring Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a disk and CPU monitoring script for Ubuntu. Use thresholds I can configure, write logs to a chosen path, and avoid sending alerts until I approve the command.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Cron Automation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a cron-based automation plan from these requirements. Explain the schedule, file paths, permissions, logs, and the commands that require explicit approval.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a Production Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Take this shell script and harden it for production. Add strict mode, validation, logging, error handling, safe cleanup, and notes for rollback testing.

output_examples.input: Request: Create a daily backup workflow for application logs.
output_examples.output: A reviewed Bash script outline with configurable paths, timestamped archive names, retention logic, logging, and a dry-run mode.

output_examples.input: Request: Monitor a server for high CPU and disk usage.
output_examples.output: A monitoring plan with threshold variables, commands used for measurement, log output location, and optional alerting steps.

output_examples.input: Request: Turn a cleanup task into a safe cron job.
output_examples.output: A cron schedule proposal, a cleanup script checklist, permission notes, and warnings for destructive file deletion commands.

best_practices: Run every generated script in a test environment before production use.
Replace placeholders with absolute paths, approved hosts, and least-privilege service accounts.
Add dry-run modes, backups, logging, and explicit approval for sudo, deletion, cron, and remote execution.
anti_patterns: Do not run copied templates unchanged on production hosts.
Do not place plaintext database passwords in scripts or command-line arguments.
Do not use deletion, rsync --delete, cron, or sudo commands without review and rollback planning.

faq.question: Does this skill execute shell scripts automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides templates and guidance. Users or agents must choose to create and run scripts separately.

faq.question: Why is the security risk rated medium?
faq.answer: The skill contains many administrative shell commands. They are legitimate templates, but they can modify systems or delete data if misused.

faq.question: Can I use these templates on production servers?
faq.answer: Use them only after review, testing, and adaptation to your environment, permissions, logging, and rollback process.

faq.question: Which tools does this skill support?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include complete production-grade scripts?
faq.answer: It provides starting templates. You should add validation, error handling, tests, monitoring, and environment-specific safeguards.

faq.question: What commands need the most caution?
faq.answer: Review sudo, crontab, nohup, rm, rsync --delete, ssh, package installation, and service control commands before execution.
